section.title: The Offerings of the Prince
section.sectionRationale: Having named the prince's duties and boundaries in chapter 45, the vision now shows him in worship: entering, standing, bowing, and providing sacrifices on Sabbaths, New Moons, and feast days, always as a worshiper alongside the people, never as a priest at the altar. The chapter closes with the kitchens where the ordinary sacrifices of the people are cooked, holiness fenced carefully at every point.
section.thesis: Even the prince, the highest human authority in the restored land, worships as a layman under the same ordered access as everyone else, entering and leaving by the same gate as the people and offering through the priests rather than for them, a picture of true leadership that submits to God rather than presuming upon Him.
section.frameNote: Four scenes: the shut gate opened for the prince and the people on Sabbaths and New Moons; the fixed and freewill offerings the prince provides across the sacred calendar; a law preserving the people's inheritance from royal confiscation; and the kitchens at the corners of the outer court where the people's sacrifices are cooked.

units.label: The gate opened for worship
units.note: the prince enters as a worshiper among the people

verses.text: “This is what the Lord GOD says: ‘The gate of the inner court that faces east must be kept shut during the six days of work, but on the Sabbath day and on the day of the New Moon it shall be opened.

The {{nasi|prince}} is to enter from the outside through the portico of the gateway and stand by the gatepost, while the priests sacrifice his burnt offerings and peace offerings. He is to bow in worship at the threshold of the gate and then depart, but the gate must not be shut until evening.
On the Sabbaths and New Moons the people of the land are also to bow in worship before the LORD at the entrance to that gateway.
The burnt offering that the prince presents to the LORD on the Sabbath day shall be six unblemished male lambs and an unblemished ram.
The grain offering with the ram shall be one ephah, and the grain offering with the lambs shall be as much as he is able, along with a hin of oil per ephah.
On the day of the New Moon he shall offer a young, unblemished bull, six lambs, and a ram without blemish.
He is to provide a grain offering of an ephah with the bull, an ephah with the ram, and as much as he is able with the lambs, along with a hin of oil per ephah.
When the prince enters, he shall go in through the portico of the gateway, and he shall go out the same way.

units.label: One gate in, the opposite gate out
units.note: the prince among the people, freewill and daily offerings

verses.text: When the people of the land come before the LORD at the appointed feasts, whoever enters by the north gate to worship must go out by the south gate, and whoever enters by the south gate must go out by the north gate. No one is to return through the gate by which he entered, but each must go out by the opposite gate.
When the people enter, the prince shall go in with them, and when they leave, he shall leave.
At the festivals and appointed feasts, the grain offering shall be an ephah with a bull, an ephah with a ram, and as much as one is able to give with the lambs, along with a hin of oil per ephah.

When the prince makes a freewill offering to the LORD, whether a burnt offering or a peace offering, the gate facing east must be opened for him. He is to offer his burnt offering or peace offering just as he does on the Sabbath day. Then he shall go out, and the gate must be closed after he goes out.
And you shall provide an unblemished year-old lamb as a daily burnt offering to the LORD; you are to offer it every morning.

You are also to provide with it every morning a grain offering of a sixth of an ephah with a third of a hin of oil to moisten the fine flour, a grain offering to the LORD. This is a permanent statute.
Thus they shall provide the lamb, the grain offering, and the oil every morning as a regular burnt offering.’

units.label: The prince's inheritance protected
units.note: no confiscation of the people's land

verses.text: This is what the Lord GOD says: ‘If the prince gives a gift to any of his sons as an inheritance, it will belong to his descendants. It will become their property by inheritance.

But if he gives a gift from his inheritance to one of his servants, it will belong to that servant until the year of freedom; then it will revert to the prince. His inheritance belongs only to his sons; it shall be theirs.

The prince must not take any of the inheritance of the people by evicting them from their property. He is to provide an inheritance for his sons from his own property, so that none of My people will be displaced from his property.’”

units.label: The kitchens of the temple
units.note: cooking the people's sacrifices without spreading holiness

verses.text: Then the man brought me through the entrance at the side of the gate into the holy chambers facing north, which belonged to the priests, and he showed me a place there at the far western end

and said to me, “This is the place where the priests shall boil the guilt offering and the {{chatat|sin offering}}, and where they shall bake the grain offering, so that they do not bring them into the outer court and transmit holiness to the people.”
Then he brought me into the outer court and led me around to its four corners, and I saw a separate court in each of its corners.
In the four corners of the outer court there were enclosed courts, each forty cubits long and thirty cubits wide. Each of the four corner areas had the same dimensions.
Around the inside of each of the four courts was a row of masonry with ovens built at the base of the walls on all sides.
And he said to me, “These are the kitchens where those who minister at the temple will cook the {{zebach|sacrifices}} offered by the people.”

nasi.gloss: Prince, chief, one lifted up. In this chapter the nasi is shown worshiping exactly as a layman does, entering and leaving by the same route as the people, never crossing into priestly ministry at the altar itself.

chatat.gloss: Sin offering, purification offering. Even this cooked in a separate, guarded kitchen, so its holiness would not spread carelessly to the ordinary people in the outer court.

zebach.gloss: Sacrifice, a slaughtered offering, often eaten in fellowship. These are the sacrifices brought by the ordinary worshiper, cooked in the corner kitchens rather than in the holy chambers reserved for the priests' portion.

basic.context: Chapter 45 fixed the prince's territory and his duty to supply offerings; chapter 46 shows him actually worshiping. On Sabbaths and New Moons the inner east gate, otherwise shut, opens so the prince may stand at its threshold while the priests sacrifice on his behalf, and the ordinary people bow at the same gate. The prince enters and leaves exactly as he came, never through the gate reserved for the LORD alone in chapter 44, and when the whole community gathers for the feasts, he goes in and out with the people rather than above them. The chapter also protects ordinary Israelites from royal land grabs, echoing the same concern for justice raised in chapter 45, and closes with the practical detail of corner kitchens where the people's own sacrifices are cooked, holiness carefully fenced from spreading where it should not.
basic.hermeneutics: The vision continues to describe rather than legislate; its architectural and ritual precision serves a theological point rather than demanding a literal future replication. The abiding truth is that true leadership, even at its highest earthly level, submits to the same access to God that ordinary worshipers have, and does not presume to stand where only the LORD, or His priests, may stand.
basic.originalLanguages: The nasi, prince, is carefully distinguished throughout from a melek, king; here he worships like anyone else, standing at a threshold rather than at an altar. Even the chatat, sin offering, must be cooked away from the people so its holiness does not spread where it should not, and the zebach, the ordinary worshiper's sacrifice, has its own guarded kitchen in the outer court.
basic.typology: The prince's worship alongside, never above, his people anticipates the true King who, though He alone deserved to stand anywhere, humbled Himself and was numbered among sinners at His baptism and His death. The careful containment of holiness, so it does not leak carelessly into ordinary life, anticipates the moment holiness itself would go out freely to touch and heal rather than merely to be guarded, in the ministry of Jesus.
basic.christ: Philippians 2:6-8 says that though He existed in the form of God, Christ did not consider equality with God as something to be grasped, but emptied Himself, taking the form of a servant, and being found in the likeness of man, He humbled Himself. The prince who enters by the ordinary gate and leaves by the ordinary way, never presuming on the LORD's own entrance, images this same humility: true, divinely appointed authority does not grasp at access it has not been given, a pattern the world's true King fulfilled perfectly, and one He now invites His people to imitate.
basic.god: God cares about the details of ordinary life and worship, not only the grand vision of glory in chapter 43. He fences even the cooking of sacrifices so holiness spreads exactly as intended, and He holds His appointed leader to the same worship pattern as the people he serves, showing that no human office exempts anyone from ordered, humble access to a holy God.
basic.crossReferences: Exodus 21:2 and Leviticus 25:10 give the background for the year of freedom mentioned in 46:17, the year when servants and property are released back. 1 Kings 21 tells the story of King Ahab seizing Naboth's inheritance, the very abuse of royal power that 46:18 forbids outright. Philippians 2:6-8 shows the ultimate pattern of humble, non-grasping authority fulfilled in Christ. Ezekiel 44:1-3 sets the earlier restriction on the LORD's own gate that this chapter's prince still respects.
basic.oneStory: Scripture keeps testing whether human rulers will grasp power that is not theirs or serve humbly under God's own reign. Israel's kings so often failed that test, seizing land like Ahab and defiling worship like the kings mourned in chapter 43. Here the coming prince is pictured worshiping humbly among his people, and that picture is fulfilled in Jesus, the true King who did not grasp equality with God but humbled Himself, and who now leads His people not by seizing what is theirs but by giving up everything that is His.

context.literary: Continues directly from chapter 45's fixing of the prince's territory and offerings, now showing him in the act of worship, and closes the section on ordered access before the vision turns outward to the river flowing from the temple in chapter 47.
context.historical: Speaks directly into the memory of kings like Ahab who seized land from ordinary Israelites, and priest-kings who blurred lines between royal and priestly authority; this chapter fences the prince firmly within lay worship and forbids exactly the land seizures Israel's history had already seen.
context.canonical: Draws on Exodus and Leviticus law concerning the year of release, and on the narrative memory of 1 Kings 21, while anticipating the New Testament's portrait of Christ's humility in Philippians 2 as the final answer to what faithful, non-grasping rule looks like.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To show that in the restored order the highest human authority worships as a layman, submitting to the same gates and offerings as the people, and to protect ordinary Israelites from the abuses of royal power Israel had already suffered under its kings.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter describes a specific vision of worship order and civic protection for a future prince; it is not a manual mandating identical gate ceremonies today. Its abiding truth is that legitimate authority submits to God's ordered access rather than presuming upon it, and that leaders must never seize what belongs to those they serve.

debates.question: Why does the prince offer through the priests rather than himself, when kings like David and Solomon are shown offering sacrifices personally?

views.label: A tightened restoration of priestly boundaries
views.case: This vision may deliberately correct the blurred lines of the monarchy period, when kings sometimes acted in priestly roles, by restricting the prince strictly to lay worship through the appointed priests.

views.label: Consistent with the Law's original intent
views.case: Others see this simply enforcing what the Law always intended, priestly sacrifice reserved for Aaron's line, so any earlier royal overreach was itself an exception rather than the ordained pattern.

debates.bearing: Either way, the passage insists that even highest human authority does not have unmediated access to the altar, a boundary only Christ, our true High Priest and King, transcends.

study.originalLanguages: The nasi, prince, is again carefully distinct from a melek, king, worshiping here as a layman standing at a threshold rather than serving at an altar. Even the chatat, sin offering, must be cooked in a guarded location so its holiness is not spread where it should not go, and the zebach, the ordinary worshiper's sacrifice, is cooked in its own corner kitchen in the outer court.

typology.type: The prince entering and leaving by the ordinary gate, never the LORD's own gate
typology.fulfillment: Anticipates Christ, who though equal with God did not grasp that equality but humbled Himself, taking the form of a servant, Philippians 2:6-8.

typology.type: Holiness carefully contained in guarded kitchens and chambers
typology.fulfillment: Anticipates the ministry of Jesus, in whom holiness moved outward to touch and heal the unclean rather than remaining merely fenced and guarded.

study.christ: Philippians 2:6-8 says that though Christ existed in the form of God, He did not consider equality with God as something to be grasped, but emptied Himself, taking the form of a servant, and being found in the likeness of man, He humbled Himself by becoming obedient to the point of death, even death on a cross. The prince who enters and leaves by the people's own gate, never presuming on the LORD's exclusive entrance, pictures exactly this kind of humility: real, God-given authority that refuses to grasp at what has not been given, a pattern only Christ, the world's true King, has ever fulfilled perfectly.
study.god: God attends to the smallest details of ordinary worship, from cooking arrangements to gate traffic, and He holds His own appointed prince to the same worship pattern as everyone else, refusing to let any human office claim unmediated access to His presence. His holiness is precious enough to be carefully fenced, and His justice is firm enough to forbid any ruler from seizing what belongs to His people.

crossReferences.note: Give the background for the year of freedom, or year of jubilee, referenced in Ezekiel 46:17, when servants and property revert.
Tells of King Ahab seizing Naboth's vineyard, the exact abuse of royal power that Ezekiel 46:18 explicitly forbids.
Shows Christ's ultimate pattern of humble, non-grasping authority, fulfilling what this chapter's prince only pictures.
Sets the earlier restriction on the LORD's own east gate, which this chapter's prince continues to respect by using the ordinary gate instead.

study.oneStory: Scripture repeatedly tests whether human rulers will grasp power that is not theirs or serve humbly under God's reign, and Israel's kings, seizing land like Ahab and blurring sacred boundaries, so often failed that test. Here the coming prince is pictured worshiping humbly alongside his people, entering and leaving as they do. That picture finds its full answer in Jesus, the true King who did not grasp equality with God but emptied Himself, and who now leads His people not by taking what is theirs but by giving everything that was His.

quiz.q: How does the prince enter and leave the inner east gate on the Sabbath?

options.text: He enters through the portico of the gateway and stands by the gatepost, then departs the same way
options.note: 46:2 and 46:8 describe exactly this pattern of entry and exit.

options.text: He enters through the gate reserved only for the LORD in chapter 44
options.note: That gate remains permanently shut to everyone, including the prince, per 44:2.

options.text: He is carried in by the priests as part of the ceremony
options.note: The text describes him entering and standing on his own, not being carried.

options.text: He offers the sacrifice himself at the altar before departing
options.note: The priests sacrifice on his behalf, 46:2; the prince does not personally minister at the altar.

quiz.q: What rule governs the people's movement through the gates during the appointed feasts?

options.text: Whoever enters by one gate must leave by the opposite gate, not returning the way they came
options.note: 46:9 states this rule precisely, north to south and south to north.

options.text: Everyone must enter and exit by the east gate only
options.note: The east gate belongs to the LORD and the prince; the people use the north and south gates.

options.text: Men and women use separate, permanently assigned gates
options.note: The distinction given is about entry versus exit direction, not gender-assigned gates.

options.text: The order of entry depends on tribal rank
options.note: No tribal ranking of gate use is given; the rule is about direction of travel, not tribe.

quiz.q: What does 46:18 explicitly forbid the prince from doing?

options.text: Taking any of the inheritance of the people by evicting them from their property
options.note: 46:18 states this prohibition directly, protecting ordinary Israelites' land.

options.text: Offering freewill offerings on behalf of the people
options.note: Freewill offerings by the prince are described positively in 46:12, not forbidden.

options.text: Giving any gift to his own sons
options.note: 46:16 explicitly permits and protects such gifts as a lasting inheritance for his sons.

options.text: Entering the temple on ordinary working days
options.note: The gate is simply shut on working days for everyone, not a specific prohibition aimed at the prince alone.

quiz.q: Why are the sin offering and grain offering boiled and baked in a separate chamber rather than the outer court?

options.text: So that the priests do not transmit holiness to the people through contact with these offerings in the outer court
options.note: 46:20 gives this exact reason for the guarded location.

options.text: Because the outer court had no ovens or cooking facilities at all
options.note: The outer court's corners in fact contain their own kitchens, 46:21-24, for different sacrifices.

options.text: Because only Levites, and no priests, were permitted to cook
options.note: The text specifically assigns this cooking to the priests, not Levites.

options.text: To keep the meat from spoiling in the open air
options.note: The stated concern is transmitting holiness to the people, not food preservation.

quiz.q: How does Philippians 2 illuminate the prince's humble pattern of worship in this chapter?

options.text: Christ, though equal with God, did not grasp that equality but emptied Himself and humbled Himself as a servant, the pattern the prince's non-grasping worship anticipates
options.note: Philippians 2:6-8 describes this humility, which the prince's careful, ordinary access pictures in shadow.

options.text: Christ claimed the same exclusive entrance reserved for the LORD alone, unlike the prince
options.note: This misreads Philippians 2, which stresses Christ's humility and self-emptying, not a grasping for exclusive access.

options.text: Christ abolished the need for any ordered access to God whatsoever
options.note: Hebrews, not Philippians 2, addresses access to God, and even there Christ opens a way rather than abolishing order.

options.text: Christ became a priest exactly like the sons of Zadok, with no distinction
options.note: Hebrews 7 stresses Christ's priesthood is of a different, greater order than Zadok's line, not identical to it.

questions: The prince worships exactly as the people do, entering and leaving by the same gate. Where might God be calling you to set aside any assumed privilege and worship simply as one among His people?

The rule to exit by the opposite gate meant no one could linger where they entered, keeping worship moving and orderly. Is there a habit in your own worship life that has grown careless or complacent that this picture of intentional order might correct?

God explicitly protects ordinary people's inheritance from a ruler's seizure. Where do you see power being used to take rather than to give in your own sphere, and how might you use whatever influence you have differently?
The sin offering had to be handled carefully so its holiness would not spread carelessly. How does this shape your understanding of reverence in worship, even under the freedom Christ has won for us?

Christ, though equal with God, did not grasp at that equality but emptied Himself. Where in your life is God inviting you to imitate that kind of humility rather than grasping for status or recognition?

translationNotes.note: 'Bow in worship at the threshold' pictures prostration, a physical posture of submission that the prince shares with the ordinary people named in 46:3, underlining that his worship is of the same kind as theirs.
'To moisten the fine flour' describes mixing oil into the grain offering; the em dash in the source text has been rendered as a comma here without altering the meaning.

'The year of freedom' almost certainly refers to the jubilee year of Leviticus 25, when property given away reverts to its original owner, here applied specifically to protect the prince's sons from losing their permanent inheritance.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session via the makor scripture toolkit for Ezekiel 46:1-24. The single em dash at 46:14 was replaced with a comma. No em or en dashes used elsewhere; no embedded line breaks found in this chapter's text. Web search this session (Truth For Life, Desiring God, Gospel Coalition, Gospel in Life) found no sermon from the trusted cluster (Begg, Keller, Piper, Ferguson, Carson) that specifically expounds Ezekiel 46; only broad, multi-book overview messages surfaced, so sermon status is none.
